Always ask: what happens to the released capacity? Idle capacity has zero opportunity cost; profitable alternative use has significant opportunity cost.
9. Sell-or-Process-Further Decisions

When a product can be sold at an intermediate stage (called the split-off point) OR further processed into a more refined product and sold at a higher price, management must choose. The rule: process further ONLY if the additional revenue from further processing exceeds the additional cost of that processing. Costs already incurred up to the split-off point are SUNK — they are irrelevant to the decision.

A decision on whether to sell a product at the split-off point or to process it further into a more refined product before selling. Process further only if the INCREMENTAL revenue exceeds the INCREMENTAL processing cost. Joint costs incurred before the split-off point are sunk and irrelevant.

A refinery produces crude gasoline at split-off. It can be sold as-is or refined further into premium fuel.

  Selling price at split-off (crude):    $60 per barrel
  Selling price after refining (premium): $85 per barrel
  Additional processing cost:            $20 per barrel

Joint cost incurred BEFORE split-off: $40 per barrel (already spent — SUNK, IRRELEVANT).

Relevant analysis:
  Incremental revenue = $85 − $60 = $25 per barrel
  Incremental cost    = $20 per barrel
  Incremental profit  = $5 per barrel  ✓ PROCESS FURTHER

Common mistake: subtracting the joint cost from the further-processed price ($85 − $40 − $20 = $25)
and comparing to $60 − $40 = $20 would give the same answer here but is conceptually wrong.
The joint cost of $40 is sunk — it must be ignored, not allocated to the decision.

Sell-or-process-further is the classic 'sunk cost' trap on the exam. Any joint cost incurred BEFORE the split-off point is irrelevant. The ONLY question is: does the extra revenue from processing exceed the extra cost of processing? If yes, process further. If no, sell at split-off.
10. Value Chain and Value Engineering

CVP is a short-run tool because in the short run the market sets prices and costs. What customers will pay and what competitors do govern price; suppliers largely govern input costs. That leaves a company two levers: which products it supplies and in what quantity — which is what CVP analyses — plus a limited ability to push costs down through supplier concessions and value engineering.
The sequence of steps a business goes through to transform inputs such as raw materials into finished products, adding value at each process, and finally to sell those finished products to customers.

An evaluation of all the business functions in the value chain with the objective of reducing costs while still satisfying customer needs. It may lead to design improvements, changes to materials specifications, or modifications to manufacturing methods.

4-5.l: Value Engineering Moving the Breakeven Point
4-5.v:
A product sells for $60 with variable cost $35 and fixed costs of $150,000.
  UCM = $25   ->   breakeven = $150,000 / $25 = 6,000 units

A value engineering review finds that a redesigned housing uses less material and
removes one assembly step, without any change customers can detect:
  Variable cost falls from $35 to $31   ->   new UCM = $29
  New breakeven = $150,000 / $29 = 5,173 units

The company did not raise its price, did not cut a feature, and did not sell one
extra unit — yet it now needs 827 fewer units to break even, and every unit above
that point earns $4 more. This is the lever CVP itself cannot pull.

Keep the two ideas distinct. The value CHAIN is the map — the sequence of value-adding steps from raw material to customer. Value ENGINEERING is the activity performed on that map — reviewing every function in it to strip out cost while keeping the product acceptable to the customer. The exam tests the definitions far more often than any calculation.

v: Unit CM = Selling Price − Variable Cost per unit. CM Ratio = UCM ÷ Selling Price.
Breakeven Units = Fixed Costs ÷ UCM. Breakeven Revenue = Fixed Costs ÷ CM Ratio.
A cost is relevant only if it is BOTH future AND differs between alternatives. Sunk costs are NEVER relevant, regardless of size.
Opportunity cost exists only under constrained capacity. With idle capacity, accepting a new order does not require giving anything up.

CVP rests on eight assumptions. The most tested: fixed costs constant within the relevant range, unit variable cost and unit selling price constant, and a constant sales mix for a multi-product company.
